questions.q: Which Claude tier is the best default choice for most daily work in 2026?
choices: Haiku
Sonnet
Opus
Whatever the home screen recommends
questions.explain: Sonnet lives in the fast-and-capable quadrant. It handles roughly 80% of daily work — drafting, code review, summarisation — at one-fifth the cost of Opus and one-twentieth the cost of the very top tier.

questions.q: You're running a cron job that classifies 5,000 support emails a day. Which tier should you reach for, and why?
choices: Opus, because Opus is the best
Sonnet, because it's the safe default
Haiku, because volume work compounds latency and cost
Either Sonnet or Opus, they're equivalent on classification
questions.explain: Volume tasks compound the cost and latency difference. Haiku is roughly 12× cheaper than Sonnet per token and responds much faster — exactly what matters when you're doing the same thing 5,000 times a day. Classification is exactly the kind of structured task that doesn't need Opus-level reasoning.

questions.q: What's the single biggest cost mistake beginners make with the Claude family?
choices: Picking Haiku when they should have picked Sonnet
Reaching for Opus on every task out of habit
Subscribing to the Max plan before testing on pay-as-you-go
Both B and C
questions.explain: Routing everything through Opus burns a 5–60× cost premium per token, and the $200/month Max plan is wasted if you don't actually need Opus-level reasoning that often. The fix is the same for both: start at Sonnet, set a hard spend cap, escalate only when you hit a wall, downgrade when you realise the wall was you being timid.

questions.q: True or false: 'Opus 4.8' and 'Opus' are interchangeable ways to refer to the same thing.
choices: True — the version is just a label
False — Opus is the tier; 4.8 is the version of that tier
questions.explain: Tier is the speed/cost slot (Haiku / Sonnet / Opus). Version is which generation of that tier you're calling (4.6, 4.7, 4.8 — these numbers update quarterly). The tier choice swings your bill by 5–60×; the version choice swings it by maybe 1.2×. Tier first, version second.

questions.q: You need an LLM to draft a launch announcement that absolutely has to land in the right tone on the first try. Which tier?
choices: Haiku
Sonnet
Opus
questions.explain: This is a one-shot where 'good enough' fails. Opus's better calibration and willingness to rewrite until the tone reads right makes it the right tool here — assuming you can afford the higher per-token cost. For everything else, start at Sonnet.

questions.q: Why does the cost gap between Opus and Sonnet matter more than the gap between Sonnet versions?
choices: Versions change pricing more often than tiers do
The cross-tier cost gap is roughly 5×; cross-version is at most ~1.2×
Anthropic only bills by version, not by tier
It doesn't — they're roughly the same size
questions.explain: Rough per-million-token cost: Haiku ≈ $1.25 out, Sonnet ≈ $15 out, Opus ≈ $75 out. The tier-to-tier move is a 5–60× swing in your bill. Version-to-version inside a tier is at most about 1.2×. Optimise tier first, version second.
